title: A Schizophrenia Drug Patients Can Stick With: Heather Turner on The Long Run - TimmermanReport.com
gist: Podcast-episode intro profiling Heather Turner, CEO of LB Pharmaceuticals, whose lead candidate LB102 (a once-daily, blood-brain-barrier-optimized methylated derivative of amisulpride) is in late-stage trials for schizophrenia and other serious mental health disorders; Turner previously led Carmot Therapeutics to its 2024 acquisition by Roche.

insights.claim: LB Pharmaceuticals is running late-stage clinical trials for LB102, a methylated derivative of amisulpride (a benzamide antipsychotic approved in more than 50 countries) that was never brought to the US market before amisulpride went generic there.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Watch LB102's late-stage trial readouts: it targets a US market gap left by amisulpride's absence there, despite the parent compound's long approval history abroad.
insights.quote: It's a methylated derivative of amisulpride, a benzamide antipsychotic. It's approved in more than 50 countries, but never made it to the market in the US before going generic.

insights.claim: The chemical modification in LB102 is designed to more efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier than amisulpride, enabling lower doses and once-daily dosing instead of amisulpride's twice-daily regimen, aimed at improving medication adherence -a particular challenge in mental health treatment.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: An adherence-focused reformulation (lower dose, once-daily) is a reliable value-creation lever in CNS drug development; if borne out clinically, it could support differentiated positioning versus generic amisulpride abroad and existing US antipsychotics.
insights.quote: The LB modification is supposed to make the compound more efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier, so it can be given in lower doses, and once a day instead of twice a day like with amisulpride.

themes.theme: CEO track record and prior exit

insights.claim: Heather Turner previously served as CEO of Carmot Therapeutics, developer of GLP-1 receptor agonist-based obesity treatments, which Roche acquired in 2024 for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestone payments.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Turner's proven exit execution in the GLP-1/obesity space is a relevant signal for LB Pharmaceuticals' own partnership or exit potential in CNS; the $3.1B total Roche deal underscores continued big-pharma appetite for differentiated metabolic assets.
insights.quote: Carmot was acquired by Roche for $2.7 billion upfront, plus $400 million in milestone payments, in 2024.
insights.locator: paragraph on Heather's prior CEO role

insights.claim: Heather Turner entered the biotech industry from a career in law rather than a scientific or operating background before becoming a two-time biotech CEO.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Minor color on leadership-pipeline diversity in biotech C-suites; not directly actionable but relevant context for talent and governance watchers.
insights.locator: near end, on Heather's background in law
